verdicts.note: Score and citations check out, but the highlights line 'Secured over $60 million in Community Project (earmark) funding' states as fact what the dossier attributes as a campaign claim ('Says she secured over $60 million' [S1]). Highlight should keep the 'says' hedge, as the insulin/Medicare highlight does.

The only evidence is a single earmark line: body cameras and a rapid DNA system for King County law enforcement [S1]. Body cameras are at least as associated with police accountability as with the enforcement pole, and the dossier explicitly contains no broader safety platform. Inferring an enforcement-lean (-1) from one funding deliverable is not a directional position; this score should be dropped rather than kept at low confidence.

-2 (fully audit-and-restrain) is exaggerated. Her dossier supports the direction via biography — six years of VA fraud-prevention work presented as insider knowledge of waste [S1][S2] — but her listed positions (cost of living, small business, safe streets, schools, veterans) contain no explicit audit-first or restrain-spending platform. The scorer's claim that she 'centers her candidacy on rooting out government waste' overstates what the dossier says. Biography-plus-framing supports -1, not the maximal pole.

Evidence is identical in thinness to Meline's ('strengthen public safety' / 'Safe Streets' label with no detail [S1][S2]), which the scorer correctly rated low with 'direction inferred, no detailed safety platform.' Same evidence should get the same confidence: low, not medium.

Direction is an indirect inference. 'Faith, freedom, family, personal responsibility' is values branding, and 'restore focus on core academics' with 'parental engagement themes' [S1][S2] is soft; the dossier records no position on reproductive rights, LGBTQ+ issues, or curricula content. Under the confidence rules, indirect inference is low, not medium.

Direction is right, but +2 (fully pole_b) rests on one generic pamphlet phrase — 'a progressive tax system on higher incomes' [S1] — with no specific proposal (no millionaires tax, wealth tax, or rate plan). A single unelaborated phrase supports +1, not the maximal position.

+2 (fully new-voices) is inconsistent with the evidence and with the scorer's own treatment of Hagglund. Arnold is a career federal employee who has run for this same seat every cycle since 2006 [S3][S5] — a two-decade candidate history is the opposite of 'generational change' or fresh perspective, even though he holds no office. Hagglund was tempered to +1 partly for repeat candidacies; Arnold's is longer. +1 fits.

The axis poles turn on replacing private insurance with a public system versus strengthening the current one. The dossier evidence is the bare pamphlet phrase 'universal healthcare' [S1] with no mechanism — universal coverage does not necessarily mean single-payer replacement. That supports +1, not the maximal +2.

The cited plank — 'empowering parents and local communities over bureaucracies' in education [S2] — targets bureaucracy, not curricula content, and the dossier records no position on reproductive rights, gender issues, or CRT. Reading a social-axis stance out of an anti-bureaucracy education line is indirect inference: low confidence, not medium.

-2 overstates. His accountability pillar demanding federal audits of waste [S1][S2] is squarely pole_a, but pole_a is 'restraint before any new spending' and he simultaneously proposes federal investments in reliable energy and trades apprenticeships [S1][S2]. Audit-focused but not spending-restraint-maximal: -1.

No climate position exists in the dossier — the basis itself admits this. 'Investments in reliable energy' appears in a jobs/economy plank alongside apprenticeships and 'AI proof jobs' [S1][S2]; it says nothing about carbon pricing, climate policy, or trading affordability against climate action. Direction is manufactured from a non-climate statement; drop the score.

Running with 'no establishment backing, no flashy ads, no big money' [S1] describes his circumstances (he raised ~$18,000 [S3]), not a reform position — nothing in the dossier shows he refuses PAC money as a matter of principle or supports term limits, RCV, dark-money bans, or any structural reform. The scorer's own basis concedes 'without specific structural-reform proposals.' Underdog rhetoric is not evidence on this axis; drop the score.
